title: Method or property accessed on null-valued expression — RuntimeException in event handler
description: Calling a method or accessing a property on a PowerShell variable that is $null throws System.Management.Automation.RuntimeException: 'You cannot call a method on a null-valued expression.' This is especially dangerous in WinForms button-click event handlers where closured variables (controls, data lists, result objects) may not yet be initialised, or where a lookup (e.g. .Controls.Find(), Where-Object, Get-Item) returned $null silently. Unlike NullReferenceException in C#, PS 5.1 gives no line-number in the compiled lambda stack frame, making diagnosis very slow. PS 7 provides the ?. null-conditional operator, but that operator is blocked by SIN-PATTERN-005 — PS 5.1 targets must use explicit $null -ne guards instead.
detection_method: Regex scan for [0] array-index access immediately followed by a property or method call, and for .Controls.Find() result immediately dereferenced — both are classic null sources in WinForms PS code
known_instances: SIN-20260403-NULL-METHOD-CALL: WinForms Button.OnClick event handler (lambda_method1396) — System.Management.Automation.RuntimeException: You cannot call a method on a null-valued expression.
exception_message: System.Management.Automation.RuntimeException: You cannot call a method on a null-valued expression.
preventionRule:
In PS 5.1 there is no null-conditional operator (that is PS 7+ and blocked by P005). Always guard every method call or property access on a variable that may be $null:
  if ($null -ne $obj) { $obj.Method() }
In WinForms event handlers:
  1. Declare control variables in $Script: scope so all handlers share the same reference.
  2. Wrap the entire handler body in try/catch and log with Write-AppLog.
  3. Never index into an array result ([0]) without first checking @($arr).Count -gt 0.
  4. Never dereference a .Controls.Find() result without null-check: $ctrl = $form.Controls.Find('name',$true); if ($null -ne $ctrl -and @($ctrl).Count -gt 0) { $ctrl[0].Text }.
remedy:
# BEFORE (SIN)
$ctrl = $form.Controls.Find('myBtn', $true)[0]
$ctrl.Enabled = $false   # crashes if Find returned empty array

# AFTER (safe)
$found = @($form.Controls.Find('myBtn', $true))
if ($found.Count -gt 0 -and $null -ne $found[0]) {
    $found[0].Enabled = $false
}

# General pattern
# BEFORE
$result = Get-Something
$result.Method()

# AFTER
$result = Get-Something
if ($null -ne $result) {
    $result.Method()
}
trusted_fix_notes: Pattern newly registered from live runtime exception in WinForms button handler. Remedy not yet applied to known instance.
